narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles like yours from past students. The model estimated a 47% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass with high confidence. The primary driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of about 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance in school placed you in the lowest group compared to peers. This factor had the strongest influence, outweighing others and tipping the scales toward concern about bar exam success. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA strongly pulled in the opposite direction, suggesting the model saw it as evidence of your potential to pass, counteracting some of the risk. Similarly, your lower-middle family income bracket contributed favorably, as the model associates it with resilience or motivation that has helped similar students succeed on the bar. Your LSAT score of 28, notably below the average of 36.7, and your Year 1 decile of 3, also under the typical 5.7, played supporting roles in the overall assessment, though less decisively than the top three factors. Full-time enrollment plans aligned with the vast majority of students, so it didn't sway the outcome much. Compared to typical admitted applicants, your profile shows strengths in prior college work but flags in school deciles and test scores that mirrored patterns of past bar failures. This isn't a judgment on your abilities as an individual—many overcome similar hurdles—but the model weighs these patterns heavily to minimize risks for the program. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep, and reapplying with updated credentials that address these key areas. Schools value persistence, and targeted improvements here could shift your prediction dramatically in your favor.
